message.content:
Adversaries may exploit file transfer protocols (FTP, FTPS, TFTP) to communicate covertly within a victim network, blending malicious traffic with legitimate data transfers to evade detection. These protocols, commonly used for transferring files, offer numerous fields and headers where data can be concealed, and adversaries may embed commands and their results within the protocol traffic. This technique has been employed by various threat actors, including APT41, Attor, CARROTBALL, and others, for command and control (C2) communications and to download additional malware.

The potential impact includes unauthorized data exfiltration, persistent C2 communication, and further malware deployment, all while mimicking normal network traffic to avoid detection. Common exploitation methods involve embedding malicious commands within protocol traffic and using the protocols to transfer files containing concealed data.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and analyzing network traffic for anomalies, implementing strict access controls and authentication for file transfer services, and employing network segmentation to limit the spread of malicious activity. Additionally, using intrusion detection systems (IDS) and intrusion prevention systems (IPS) to detect and block suspicious file transfer activities can help mitigate the risks associated with this technique.
